text.title: Minimum FS tool surface
text.summary: Four tools — write_file, read_file, ls, grep — are sufficient; avoid wrapping every operation in its own tool.

rule.statement: Expose exactly four FS tools to the agent — write_file(path, content), read_file(path, offset?, limit?), ls(path?), grep(pattern, path?) — and route every offload/recall through them.
rule.rationale: Four primitives compose like Unix utilities; adding move/copy/append separately bloats the tool list and makes the agent pick wrong tools (see bundle-vs-split-tools at ~25 tools).

example.note: Default limit forces streaming — the agent never accidentally re-loads a 50k-token file in full.

rule.statement: Use a stable directory taxonomy — search/, docs/, plan/, scratch/ — and let SKILL or system prompt list it; never let the agent invent ad-hoc top-level folders per call.
rule.rationale: A predictable taxonomy makes ls a useful navigation aid; ad-hoc paths force the agent to remember names instead of discovering them.

list.item: search/ — raw search results (one file per query).
docs/ — fetched URLs and document downloads.
plan/ — TODO files, task graphs, intermediate plans.
scratch/ — agent's own notes between turns.

section.title: Persistence

rule.statement: The FS backing store MUST persist into the same checkpointer as conversation state, so resume-after-failure recovers files alongside messages.
rule.rationale: If files survive but messages don't (or vice versa) the agent wakes up with dangling references — pointers to artifacts it can no longer interpret, or summaries of files that no longer exist.

antipattern.description: FS in tmpfs / per-process RAM, conversation in Postgres — process restart leaves the agent with file paths that 404.
antipattern.reason: Memory model splits across two persistence boundaries; agent's references decay independently of its own awareness of them.

section.reference: Deep Agents — checkpointer integration with FS state.
